details.overview: The book of Genesis is the book of beginnings – it tells of the creation of the world, humankind's fall into sin, and God's plan to save humanity. It shows how God chose Abraham and his descendants to form a nation through whom the Savior would come. The book teaches us about God's power, goodness, and faithfulness.
authorFacts: Led the Israelites out of Egypt
Received the 10 Commandments from God on Mount Sinai
Wrote the first five books of the Bible (the Pentateuch)
Lived for 120 years
additionalFacts: Moses was the youngest of three siblings
His sister, Miriam, was old enough to watch what would happen to her baby brother
His brother, Aaron, was three years older
Grew up in Pharaoh's court but chose his own people

details.overview: The book of Exodus tells of courage, faith, and stunning miracles. It shows us how God frees His people from slavery and leads them through the wilderness to the promised land. The book teaches that God hears our cries and comes to our aid even when the situation seems hopeless. The 10 Commandments give us moral guidance for living and show God's love for us.

details.overview: The book of Leviticus is a practical guide to holy living. It teaches how to approach a holy God through sacrifices and consecration. The book shows that God desires to live among His people, but sin separates us from Him. The sacrificial system points to the coming Savior who will bring ultimate atonement.

additionalFacts: The book primarily contains direct instructions from God

Despite the people's rebellion and complaining, God remained faithful to them and eventually brought them to the border of the promised land.

additionalFacts: The book contains two censuses - at the beginning and the end
12 spies were sent to explore the land of Canaan

details.overview: The book of Deuteronomy contains Moses' farewell speeches to the new generation before entering the promised land. He recounts God's faithfulness, reiterates the laws, and encourages the people to love God with all their heart. It is a call to choose life and blessing, not death and curse.

Delivered three major speeches to the people
Repeated and explained the laws to the new generation
Died before entering the promised land
additionalFacts: Moses saw the promised land from Mount Nebo
God buried him Himself; no one knows where his grave is
Joshua was appointed his successor
The book ends with the verse: 'No prophet has arisen in Israel like Moses'

details.overview: Joshua is a symbol of courage and faith. After Moses' death, he took on the responsibility of leading the Israelites into the promised land. His story teaches us that God provides strength and courage when we trust Him. Joshua did not fear challenges or adversaries, for he knew that God was with him. He is an example for those facing daunting tasks - with faith and courage, the impossible can be achieved.
authorFacts: Joshua (Heb. Yehoshua – 'The Lord is salvation') was the son of Nun, of the tribe of Ephraim, and Moses' closest assistant and servant from the beginning of the wilderness journey (Ex 24:13; 33:11)
His original name was Hoshea, but Moses gave him the new name Joshua, which refers to his role in saving the people (Num 13:16)
Moses publicly consecrated him as his successor, laying hands on him and giving him the fullness of the Spirit (Num 27:18–23; Dt 34:9)

Joshua is traditionally considered the primary author of the book – he himself recorded at least the words of the covenant renewal at Shechem (Jos 24:26); the concluding parts (e.g., his death) were added later by others, probably the priests Eleazar and Phinehas
The book is written from an eyewitness perspective – 'we' and 'to this day' forms (e.g., Jos 5:1; 6:25) indicate that the author lived during the described events
additionalFacts: Joshua was one of the 12 spies who explored the land of Canaan – only he and Caleb trusted God and were rewarded by reaching the Promised Land (Num 14:6–9, 30)
He led the first Israelite victory against Amalek at Rephidim, while Moses prayed on the mountaintop (Ex 17:8–13)
Joshua was a military leader, prophet, and spiritual leader in one person – under his leadership, the land was divided by lot among the twelve tribes
He lived to be 110 years old and was buried in his inheritance at Timnath-Serah in the hill country of Ephraim (Jos 24:29–30)
His famous testimony 'As for me and my household, we will serve the Lord' (Jos 24:15) has become one of the most well-known declarations of faith in the Old Testament
In the New Testament, Joshua is compared as a type of Jesus – both names mean 'The Lord saves,' and both lead the people into an inheritance (Heb 4:8)

details.overview: The book of Judges shows what happens when a people turn away from God. A cycle repeats: the people sin, fall under enemies, cry out to God, and God sends a deliverer – a judge. The book teaches that God is always ready to save those who turn to Him.

Was a prophet and priest
Anointed Saul and David as kings
Led Israel as the last judge
additionalFacts: There are 12 judges in the book
Samson was the physically strongest judge
Deborah was the only female judge
Gideon, with 300 men, defeated 135,000 enemies

details.overview: The book of Ruth is a beautiful story of love and loyalty set in the dark times of the Judges. Ruth, a Moabite woman, remains loyal to her mother-in-law Naomi and finds a new life in Israel. She marries Boaz and becomes the great-grandmother of King David. This shows that God's grace extends to all nations.
authorFacts: Author is unknown
Written after David became king
Emphasized God's providence
Showed that Moabites could belong to God's people
additionalFacts: Ruth was one of two Bible books named after a woman
Ruth is mentioned in Jesus' genealogy
The story takes place during the time of the Judges
Boaz was the son of Rahab

Other prophets completed it after his death
Documented Israel's first kings
Was a prophet, priest, and judge
additionalFacts: Samuel was born in answer to his mother Hannah's prayer
He anointed two kings: Saul and David
Saul lost the kingdom due to disobedience

details.overview: The first book of Kings tells of Solomon's reign and the division of the kingdom. Solomon, the wisest man of all time, built the temple but later fell into idolatry. After his death, the kingdom split into two - Israel in the north and Judah in the south.
authorFacts: Author is unknown, tradition attributes it to Jeremiah
Wrote in Babylonian captivity
Documented the kings' comparison to God's law

additionalFacts: Solomon asked for wisdom, not wealth or power

Wrote during or immediately after the exile

additionalFacts: Elisha performed a double portion of miracles compared to Elijah
The northern kingdom of Israel fell to Assyria in 722 BC
The southern kingdom of Judah fell to Babylon in 586 BC

details.overview: 2 Chronicles focuses on the history of the kings of Judah from Solomon to the exile. Unlike the book of Kings, which documents both kings of Israel and Judah, this book focuses only on the kings of Judah and the temple. The book ends with hope - Cyrus's decree, which allowed the Jews to return home.

Wrote for the Jews who returned from exile
Focused on the temple and Jerusalem

details.overview: The book of Ezra tells how the Jews returned from Babylonian exile and rebuilt the temple. Cyrus's decree allowed them to return, but the journey was difficult. Ezra, a priest and scribe, led a spiritual revival and taught the people God's law.
authorFacts: Ezra was a priest and scribe

details.overview: The book of Nehemiah tells how the walls of Jerusalem were rebuilt in 52 days. Nehemiah, cupbearer to the Persian king, received permission to go to Jerusalem and lead the restoration of the walls. Despite attacks from adversaries and internal problems, he successfully completed the work.

details.overview: The Book of Job deals with one of humanity's oldest questions: why do the righteous suffer? Job, a righteous man, loses everything—his possessions, his children, his health. His friends accuse him of sin, but Job holds onto his faith. At the end of the book, God Himself appears and shows His greatness. Job learns to trust, even without understanding.

details.overview: Psalms are the songs and prayers of the Bible—a mirror of the human soul. They express joy, sorrow, anger, fear, hope, and gratitude. Every emotion a human feels finds its words here. Psalms teach how to honestly communicate with God—not only with thanks, but also with questions and doubts.

details.overview: Ecclesiastes is a book about the meaning of life. Solomon, who had experienced everything—wealth, pleasure, wisdom, labor—comes to the conclusion: all is vanity without God. The book teaches that true contentment comes only from a close relationship with God, not from earthly things.

details.overview: Isaiah is the greatest prophet of the Old Testament—he is called the 'evangelist in the Old Testament'. His book contains the most accurate prophecies about the Messiah: His virgin birth, His suffering for us, and His eternal reign. Isaiah proclaims both judgment and hope.

details.overview: Ezekiel was a prophet during the Babylonian captivity. He received strange visions – of God's throne, dry bones coming to life, and a future temple. His message: God has not abandoned His people, even in exile. His Spirit will restore them and give them a new heart.

details.overview: Daniel is the story of a young man who kept his faith even in a foreign land. His book contains powerful prophetic visions of world empires and the coming of the Messiah.

details.overview: The churches in Galatia had been attacked by false teachers who said: faith in Jesus is good, but you also need to observe Jewish laws. Paul is furious. His message: salvation comes only by grace, only through faith, only in Christ. The letter became the 'manifesto' of Luther's Reformation.
authorFacts: One of Paul's earliest letters
Written to the churches in the province of Galatia
Paul's only letter without greetings
Speaks of his conversion
additionalFacts: The basis of free salvation – faith in Christ alone
Luther's Reformation 'manifesto'
Fruit of the Spirit (Gal 5:22-23)
Famous verse: 'You are all one in Christ Jesus'

breakdowns.description: Love, joy, peace...

details.overview: The book of Ephesians reveals God's great plan – to unite all things in Christ. The first three chapters describe what God has done for us (heavenly blessings, salvation by grace). The last three show how to live in response (church, family, spiritual armor).
authorFacts: Written from Roman imprisonment
Paul had worked in Ephesus for 3 years
Tychicus carried the letter
One of the 'prison epistles'
additionalFacts: Blessings appear in one of the longest Greek sentences
Contains spiritual armor (Eph 6)
Teaching on marriage as a picture of Christ and the church
Salvation by grace through faith (Eph 2:8-9)

breakdowns.title: Salvation by Grace (ch. 2)

breakdowns.description: One Lord, one faith, one baptism

breakdowns.description: Put on the whole armor of God

details.overview: This is Paul's letter of joy – the word 'joy' appears 16 times, despite Paul being in prison. The Philippian church was his favorite, sending him aid. He thanks them and teaches: rejoice in the Lord always, do not worry, think about what is true and lovely.
authorFacts: Written from Roman imprisonment
Philippi was the first European church
Paul had baptized Lydia there
Epaphroditus brought the letter back to the church
additionalFacts: The word 'joy' appears 16 times
Contains the Christ hymn (Phil 2:5-11)
Famous verse: 'I can do all things through Christ' (Phil 4:13)
Teaches about peace that surpasses all understanding

breakdowns.title: Rejoice Always (ch. 4)
breakdowns.description: The peace of the Lord will guard your hearts

details.overview: Colossae was being infiltrated by false teachings – angel worship, mystical experiences, asceticism. Paul responds: Christ is above all. In Him dwells the fullness of the Godhead. You need nothing more than Christ. The letter contains a powerful hymn to the supremacy of Christ (Col 1:15-20).
authorFacts: Written from Roman imprisonment
Paul had not visited Colossae
Epaphras was the founder of the church
The letter was sent with Onesimus
additionalFacts: Hymn to the supremacy of Christ (Col 1:15-20)
Contains household codes
Warns against gnosticism
Companion to the letter to Philemon

details.overview: One of Paul's earliest letters, written to the church in Thessalonica, which had suffered persecution. Paul encourages them to persevere and teaches about the return of Christ. Each chapter concludes with this theme – we live in expectation of the Lord's return.
authorFacts: Paul's earliest letter (possibly)
Written from Corinth
Paul had been driven out of Thessalonica
Timothy brought back good news
additionalFacts: Each chapter ends with the theme of Christ's return
Thessalonica was on a prosperous trade route
Teaches about the rapture (1 Thess 4:13-18)
A short, practical letter

details.overview: After the first letter, some Thessalonian believers thought the Day of the Lord had already come. Some had stopped working. Paul explains: certain events must happen before the Day of the Lord. And: if anyone will not work, neither shall he eat.
authorFacts: A short follow-up letter to the 1st
Written months later
Clarifies misunderstandings from the first letter
Contains practical commands
additionalFacts: Deals with the 'man of lawlessness' (antichrist)
Famous verse: 'If anyone will not work, neither shall he eat' (2 Thess 3:10)
Promises the swift return of the Lord
Teaches to hold to traditions

details.overview: Paul left young Timothy in Ephesus to care for the church. In this letter, he provides practical instructions on how to organize prayer, how to select church elders and deacons, and how to deal with different groups of people. This is a 'pastoral epistle' for church leaders.
authorFacts: One of the 'pastoral epistles'
Timothy was Paul's spiritual son
Originated from Lystra
His mother and grandmother were believers
additionalFacts: One of the three 'pastoral epistles'
Contains requirements for elders and deacons (ch. 3)
Warns against false teachings
Written during Paul's interim period of freedom

details.overview: This is Paul's last letter, written from his second Roman imprisonment before his execution. The tone of the message is personal and serious: 'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith.' He encourages Timothy to be strong and to continue his work.
authorFacts: Paul's last letter
Written from Roman imprisonment
A short time before his execution
Contains his 'last will and testament'
additionalFacts: Famous verse: 'All Scripture is God-breathed' (2 Tim 3:16)
Paul's 'testament'
Mentions many names – both friends and betrayers
Ends with the famous 'I have fought the good fight'

details.overview: Titus was Paul's co-worker in Crete. The island's reputation was poor – 'Cretans are always liars.' Paul instructs Titus: choose good leaders, teach sound doctrine, and set an example yourself. Sound doctrine leads to good works – faith and deeds go hand in hand.
authorFacts: One of the 'pastoral epistles'
Titus was of Greek background
Paul's companion on several journeys
Left in Crete to work
additionalFacts: One of the shortest Pauline epistles
Addresses the 'blessed hope' of Christ's return
Sound doctrine leads to good works
Paul's longest teaching on grace (Tit 2:11-14)

details.overview: The most personal letter of Paul – only 25 verses. Onesimus was a slave who had run away from his master, Philemon. In Rome, he met Paul and became a believer. Paul sends him back to Philemon with a plea: receive him no longer as a slave, but as a beloved brother.
authorFacts: Written from Roman imprisonment
Philemon was a wealthy believer in Colossae
Onesimus was his runaway slave
Sent along with the letter to the Colossians
additionalFacts: Shortest Pauline epistle (one chapter)
Deals with a runaway slave
Shows the impact of Christianity on the system of slavery
Onesimus later became Bishop of Ephesus

details.overview: The letter to the Hebrews shows how Christ is superior to everything – angels, Moses, priests, sacrifices. The Old Covenant system was a shadow; Christ is the reality. The letter encourages Christians who are weary in their faith: do not go back, continue in faith.
authorFacts: The author is unknown
Addressed to Christians of Jewish background
Written before the destruction of the Temple (AD 70)
One of theologically deepest books
additionalFacts: Contains the 'hall of fame' of faith heroes (Heb 11)
Teaches Christ as High Priest after the order of Melchizedek
The word 'better' appears 13 times
Warns against apostasy from the faith

details.overview: James was Jesus' brother and the leader of the Jerusalem church. His letter is practical: faith without works is dead. He discusses trials, partiality, the danger of the tongue, the power of prayer. Stylistically similar to Jesus' Sermon on the Mount.
authorFacts: Jesus' half-brother
Leader of the Jerusalem church
Was not a believer during Jesus' lifetime (Jn 7:5)
Became a believer after Jesus' resurrection
additionalFacts: One of the earliest New Testament books
Similar to the Sermon on the Mount
Famous: 'faith without works is dead' (Jas 2:17)
Teaches control of the tongue (Jas 3)

details.overview: Peter wrote to persecuted Christians in Asia Minor. His message: you are God's chosen, holy people. Suffering is part of the calling of Christian life. Christ Himself, who suffered innocently, is the example. Do not be afraid, keep strong faith.
authorFacts: Apostle and disciple of Jesus
Fisherman from Galilee
His original name was Simon
According to tradition, he was crucified upside down in Rome
additionalFacts: Written from Rome ('Babylon')
Addressed to five provinces
Mark was his scribe
Addresses suffering 12 times

details.overview: Peter's last letter, written shortly before his execution. He warns against false teachers who 'will secretly bring in destructive heresies among you'. He affirms the authority of Scripture and the certainty of Christ's return. 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise.'

Written a short time before his execution
Warns against false prophets
Addresses the inspiration of Scripture
additionalFacts: Mentions Paul's letters as Scripture

details.overview: The aged Apostle John writes a letter of love to his spiritual children. He addresses three main themes: God is light (walk in the light), God is love (love one another), and God is truth (confess Jesus as the Christ). The letter gives assurance of salvation.

Aged apostle, over 80 years old
Wrote from Ephesus
His 'little children' were the second generation

Gives assurance of salvation
Warns against Gnosticism

Brother of James
Not a believer during Jesus' lifetime
One of the shortest books of the New Testament
additionalFacts: Quotes the apocryphal Book of Enoch

Similar to 2 Peter

details.overview: The last book of the Bible. Old John was exiled on the island of Patmos. He had a powerful vision of Christ—no longer a humble sufferer, but a glorious King. The book describes the final events of the world, judgment, and the new heaven and new earth. It ends with the words: 'Come, Lord Jesus!'
authorFacts: Apostle John in old age
Exiled on the island of Patmos
Received a vision on the Lord's Day
Addressed to seven churches
additionalFacts: The last book of the Bible
Contains the number 7 over 50 times
Seven seals, trumpets, and bowls of wrath
Promises a blessing to its readers (Rev 1:3)

details.overview: The book of Judges describes the history of Israel after the death of Joshua until the time of the kings. It is a cyclical story of sin, slavery, repentance, and deliverance. God raised up leaders (judges) who freed the people from their oppressors, but the people repeatedly fell back into sin.
authorFacts: Author is unknown, tradition attributes it to Samuel
Written during the time of the kings, looking back
Documented 12 major judges
Showed Israel's need for a king
additionalFacts: The book covers a period of approximately 350 years
Deborah was the only female judge
Gideon defeated a huge army with 300 men
Samson was the most famous judge for his strength
